1: Yom Kippur (the Day of Atonement, Leviticus 23:27-28) became the most solemn day in the Jewish calendar — the only mandatory communal fast in the Torah. In Second Temple Judaism, it was the one day the high priest entered the most holy place. When the temple was destroyed in 70 CE, the entire Yom Kippur sacrificial system became inoperative — Hebrews' argument that Christ's once-for-all sacrifice supersedes the annual Yom Kippur ritual became immediately relevant to Jewish Christians watching the temple system collapse. The phrase 'once for all' (ephapax, Hebrews 9:12; Leviticus 10:10) is Hebrews' direct response to Yom Kippur's annual repetition.
2: YHWH said to Moses: Tell Aaron your brother not to come at any time into the Holy Place inside the veil, before the mercy seat that is on the ark, so that he may not die — the death-threat prohibition establishes that the most holy place is inaccessible except under the precisely controlled conditions of Yom Kippur. The restriction is specifically Aaronic (not just priestly): only the high priest may enter, and only on one day per year. In Egyptian temple practice, the innermost sanctuary containing the divine image was similarly restricted; only the highest priests (or the Pharaoh in ritual persona) could approach; the similarity in spatial restriction highlights the theological difference — the Israelite prohibition aims at protecting the covenant community from divine judgment, not protecting a divine image from human view.
3: But in this way Aaron shall come into the Holy Place: with a bull from the herd for a sin offering and a ram for a burnt offering — the high priest enters with specific prescribed animals, not freely. The bull is for Aaron's own atonement (Leviticus 16:6,11-14) before he can intercede for the congregation; the sequence personal atonement → communal atonement is structurally fundamental to Yom Kippur. No parallel Mesopotamian rite requires the officiating priest to first atone for himself before performing communal atonement; the Levitical insistence on the priest's own sinfulness is theologically distinctive.
4: He shall put on the holy linen coat and shall have the linen undergarments on his body, and he shall tie the linen sash around his waist, and wear the linen turban; these are the holy garments — Yom Kippur's vestments are strikingly simpler than the regular high-priestly garments (Exod 28: gold, blue, purple, scarlet, precious stones, bells). The linen-only set (without gold or jewels) is the same type worn by ordinary priests; the high priest's self-reduction to ordinary priestly garb for the day's atonement work signals humility and vulnerability before YHWH. Linen is also associated with the resurrection/purity in later theology; its whiteness became the dominant Yom Kippur visual symbol (cf. Isaiah 1:18).
5: And he shall take from the congregation of the people of Israel two male goats for a sin offering and one ram for a burnt offering — the two goats for the communal sin offering are the unique feature of Yom Kippur not found elsewhere in Leviticus or the ANE sacrificial corpus; both goats constitute a single sin offering (cf. Leviticus 16:5: 'two male goats for a sin offering'), but their respective fates divide: one dies as a chatat, one is sent alive to Azazel.
6: Aaron shall offer the bull as a sin offering for himself and shall make atonement for himself and for his house — the bull sin offering for Aaron and his household precedes the communal goat sin offering; the high priest's personal atonement is a prerequisite for his capacity to atone for others. This structural requirement — the priest must be atoned for before he can atone on behalf of others — is emphasized in the Yom Kippur rubric and later in Hebrews 7:27 as a limitation that the perfect High Priest transcends.
7: Then he shall take the two goats and set them before YHWH at the entrance of the tent of meeting — the two-goat presentation before YHWH at the sanctuary entrance establishes that both goats are simultaneously offered as a single complex offering; neither is simply a random animal from the flock but a paired sacrificial unit whose fate is determined by lot (Leviticus 16:8). The lot-casting gives the selection divine rather than human determination.
8: And Aaron shall cast lots over the two goats, one lot for YHWH and one lot for Azazel (goral echad la-YHWH vegoral echad la-Azazel) — the Azazel lot is one of the most debated cruxes in Leviticus. Three main interpretive streams: (1) Azazel as a place name (a rocky/remote place — related to ez goat + azal remove/disappear, 'the goat of removal'); (2) a personal/demonic name for a wilderness spirit — supported by 1 Enoch 8 and 10, which describe Azazel as a fallen angel; (3) a descriptor meaning 'complete sending-away.' The parallel structure la-YHWH/la-Azazel suggests Azazel is a personal entity parallel to YHWH, which has led most modern scholars to accept the demonic-entity interpretation — the goat is sent back to the demonic source of disorder in the wilderness, not sacrificed to it.
9: And Aaron shall present the goat on which the lot fell for YHWH and use it as a sin offering — the YHWH-lot goat follows the standard chatat procedure: slaughter, blood manipulation on the kapporet, tent of meeting, and outer altar. Its function is the ritual purification of the sanctuary from the accumulated impurities of the year.
10: But the goat on which the lot fell for Azazel shall be presented alive before YHWH to make atonement over it, that it may be sent away into the wilderness to Azazel — the Azazel-goat is not slaughtered; it is presented alive before YHWH for the confession-and-laying-on-of-hands rite (Leviticus 16:21-22) and then sent alive into the wilderness. The pairing of a slaughtered goat (sanctuary purification) and a released goat (sin-removal from the community) creates a comprehensive atonement that addresses both the sanctuary's defilement and the people's accumulated guilt.
11: Aaron shall present the bull as a sin offering for himself, and shall make atonement for himself and for his house. He shall kill the bull as a sin offering for himself — the second mention of the bull sin offering for Aaron; the repetition reinforces the structural priority of the high priest's own atonement. The bull blood will be brought inside the veil (Leviticus 16:14) in the same procedure as the goat blood (Leviticus 16:15), providing full atonement coverage for both priest and congregation.
12: And he shall take a censer full of coals of fire from the altar before YHWH, and two handfuls of sweet incense beaten small, and he shall bring it inside the veil — the incense screen is the high priest's protection in the most holy place: the incense cloud covers the kapporet (mercy seat) so that Aaron does not see it directly and die (Leviticus 16:13). In Mesopotamian ritual, priests carried incense as part of divine approach protocols — the incense as divine encounter protection is a shared ANE ritual element, but the Levitical specification that it protects the officiating priest from the divine presence above the ark is distinctive.
13: And he shall put the incense on the fire before YHWH, that the cloud of the incense may cover the mercy seat that is over the testimony, so that he does not die — the kapporet (mercy seat, or atonement-cover) is the golden lid of the ark of the covenant, flanked by the cherubim (Exodus 25:17-22). YHWH speaks from between the cherubim (Exodus 25:22); the incense cloud interposes between the high priest and this most direct divine manifestation point. The protective incense protocol implies that even the high priest, performing the most sacred act of the liturgical year, approaches YHWH under a kind of protective mediation.
14: And he shall take some of the blood of the bull and sprinkle it with his finger on the front of the mercy seat on the east side, and in front of the mercy seat he shall sprinkle the blood with his finger seven times — the blood manipulation on the kapporet proceeds in two stages: one application on the front east face, then seven sprinklings in front of the kapporet (downward, toward the floor or the front of the ark). The number seven (completeness) recurs throughout Levitical blood rituals; here it covers the full spatial surface of the most holy place with atoning blood.
15: Then he shall kill the goat of the sin offering that is for the people and bring its blood inside the veil and do with its blood as he did with the blood of the bull, sprinkling it over the mercy seat and in front of the mercy seat — the people's sin offering goat receives identical blood manipulation to the priest's bull; the spatial procedure in the most holy place is the same for both, ensuring comprehensive atonement coverage for priest and people alike. The double entry into the most holy place (for bull blood, then for goat blood) required two separate entries through the veil.
16: Thus he shall make atonement for the Holy Place, because of the uncleannesses of the people of Israel and because of their transgressions, all their sins — the explicit statement that the sanctuary itself requires atonement: the accumulated impurities and sins of the people throughout the year have penetrated and defiled even the most holy place. This understanding that sin defiles the sanctuary (rather than only the sinner) is a distinctive Levitical concept; the sanctuary must be periodically purified of this accumulated contamination, not merely the individual worshipers.
17: No one may be in the tent of meeting from the time he enters to make atonement in the Holy Place until he comes out and has made atonement for himself and for his house and for all the assembly of Israel — the total exclusion of all other persons from the tent of meeting during the high priest's entry into the most holy place creates the maximum possible sacral isolation. The high priest stands utterly alone before YHWH during the most critical moment of the liturgical year.
18: Then he shall go out to the altar that is before YHWH and make atonement for it, and shall take some of the blood of the bull and some of the blood of the goat, and put it on the horns of the altar all around — after the inner sanctuary's atonement, the outer altar receives blood from both the bull and the goat. The mixed blood (from both the priestly and communal offerings) applied to the outer altar ensures that this most publicly visible locus of worship is also covered by the day's atonement.
19: And he shall sprinkle some of the blood on it with his finger seven times, and cleanse it and consecrate it from the uncleannesses of the people of Israel — seven sprinklings on the outer altar (as on the kapporet) complete the purification cycle; ve-qiddesho (consecrate it, reconsecrate it) indicates that the altar's holiness has been compromised by the year's accumulated defilements and is now restored. The Yom Kippur procedure thus resets the entire sanctuary complex to its original consecrated state.
20: And when he has made an end of atoning for the Holy Place and the tent of meeting and the altar, he shall present the live goat — the sequence is complete: most holy place → tent of meeting → outer altar → now the live Azazel goat. The spatial movement from innermost to outermost mirrors the concentric holiness zones of the tabernacle; atonement proceeds from the most sacred center outward.
21: And Aaron shall lay both his hands on the head of the live goat and confess over it all the iniquities of the people of Israel, and all their transgressions, all their sins. And he shall put them on the head of the goat and send it away into the wilderness by the hand of a man who is in readiness — the laying of both hands (shtei yadav) on the scapegoat's head is unique in Levitical ritual (other laying-on-of-hand rites use one hand, Leviticus 1:4); the full weight of priestly identification transfers the entire community's accumulated sin-load to the goat. The verbal confession is the only instance in the sacrificial legislation where the content of the atonement act is explicitly verbal; the comprehensive three-term sin-vocabulary (avonot, pesha'im, chata'ot) covers the full range of Israelite moral failure.
22: The goat shall bear all their iniquities on itself to a remote area, and he shall let the goat go free in the wilderness — the goat literally carries (nasa) the sins; the verb is the same used for prophetic sin-bearing (Isaiah 53:4,11,12). The destination is eretz gezeirah (a cut-off land, a remote/separated region), Azazel's domain. Second Temple practice added a rocky precipice (according to Mishnah Yoma Leviticus 6:6) over which the goat was pushed to prevent its return; the Talmud mentions that a red thread tied to the precipice would turn white as a sign of divine acceptance, echoing Isaiah 1:18.
23: Then Aaron shall come into the tent of meeting and shall take off the linen garments that he put on when he went into the Holy Place and shall leave them there — the linen Yom Kippur vestments are left in the tent of meeting after the inner ceremonies; they are not taken out or reused. The spatial containment of the most-holy garments within the tent reflects the principle that what has been in the most holy place remains contaminated with its holiness and cannot return to ordinary use.
24: And he shall bathe his body in water in a holy place and put on his garments and come out and offer his burnt offering and the burnt offering of the people and make atonement for himself and for the people — the post-inner-sanctuary procedure: bathing (transition purification from the most holy contact), change to regular high-priestly garments, then burnt offerings. The personal burnt offering reaffirms Aaron's complete consecration after the day's most intimate divine encounter.
25: And the fat of the sin offering he shall burn on the altar — the fat portions of both sin offering animals (bull and goat) burn on the outer altar as the standard YHWH-portion; even on Yom Kippur the basic sacrificial mechanics remain consistent with the sacrifice laws of Lev 3–7.
26: And he who lets the goat go to Azazel shall wash his clothes and bathe his body in water, and afterward he may come into the camp — the person who led the Azazel goat to the wilderness contracts impurity from the contact with the sin-laden goat; the same laundering-and-bathing purification required after other impurity contacts (Leviticus 11:25,28,40; Leviticus 14:8; Leviticus 15:5-8) applies here. The impurity transmission confirms that the goat genuinely carried sin, not merely symbolically represented it.
27: The bull for the sin offering and the goat for the sin offering, whose blood was brought in to make atonement in the Holy Place, shall be carried outside the camp. Their skin and their flesh and their dung shall be burned up with fire — the two sin offering animals whose blood entered the most holy place are burned outside the camp, as with all inward-blood-manipulation sin offerings (Leviticus 4:12,21; Leviticus 6:30). The outside-camp burning of the most-holy sin offering animals creates a spatial parallel to the outside-camp destination of the Azazel goat; both forms of sin removal go outside.
28: And he who burns them shall wash his clothes and bathe his body in water, and afterward he may come into the camp — the person who burns the sin offering carcasses also contracts impurity requiring laundering and bathing. The systematic impurity from contact with sin-laden sacrificial materials reflects the objective weight of the sin these offerings have borne.
29: And it shall be a statute to you forever that in the seventh month, on the tenth day of the month, you shall afflict yourselves (ve-initem et-nafshoteichem) and shall do no work, either the native or the stranger who sojourns among you — Yom Kippur's two community requirements: self-affliction (innui nefesh, which rabbinic tradition interprets as fasting plus five other abstentions: bathing, anointing, leather shoes, marital relations) and complete work-prohibition. The tenth of the seventh month (Tishri) positions Yom Kippur five days before Sukkoth and ten days after Rosh Hashanah (Feast of Trumpets), creating the 'ten days of awe' structure of the high holy day season in Jewish liturgical calendar.
30: For on this day shall atonement be made for you to cleanse you. You shall be clean before YHWH from all your sins — the definitive statement of Yom Kippur's scope: mikal chatoteichem lifney YHWH titharu (from all your sins before YHWH you shall be clean). The comprehensive cleansing covers the full year's accumulated sins of the entire community; the day's procedures together accomplish what no individual sacrifice could: a complete annual reset of the covenant relationship.
31: It is a Sabbath of solemn rest to you (shabbat shabbaton hi lachem), and you shall afflict yourselves; it is a statute forever — the double Sabbath formula shabbat shabbaton (the most intense Sabbath-rest) is used only for Yom Kippur (here and Leviticus 23:32) and for the Sabbath itself (Exodus 31:15; Leviticus 35:2; Leviticus 23:3), marking Yom Kippur as the highest-intensity day of rest in the liturgical calendar.
32: And the priest who is anointed and consecrated to serve as priest in his father's place shall make atonement, wearing the holy linen garments — the provision for successor high priests ensures the Yom Kippur procedure is not dependent on Aaron personally but perpetuates through the Aaronic line; whoever holds the high-priestly office wears the linen garments and performs the prescribed rites. The phrase tachat aviv (in his father's place) establishes the hereditary succession principle for the high priesthood.
33: He shall make atonement for the holy sanctuary, and he shall make atonement for the tent of meeting and for the altar, and he shall make atonement for the priests and for all the people of the assembly — the four-part atonement summary: sanctuary, tent of meeting, altar, people. The concentric spatial sequence (from innermost sacred to the community) recapitulates the procedural order of Leviticus 16:14-19. The comprehensive scope — all sacred spaces and all people — confirms that Yom Kippur is a total-reset mechanism for the entire covenantal system.
34: And this shall be a statute forever for you, that atonement may be made for the people of Israel once in the year because of all their sins. And Moses did as YHWH commanded him — the closing statute-formula (chuqat olam) establishes Yom Kippur as a permanent annual institution; the once-per-year restriction (achat bashanah) is as theologically significant as the comprehensiveness of the atonement. The annual cycle of accumulated sin + annual atonement creates the rhythm of Israelite covenantal life until the once-for-all atonement of Christ (Hebrews 9:7,12,25-28).
